kill_reason: product overpromised: 'AI bookkeeping' was largely manual labor with unsustainable economics
summary: Raised ~$100M to automate small-business bookkeeping; the 'AI' relied heavily on human accountants behind the scenes, error rates upset customers, and the cost structure never worked. Shut down in 2020.

kill_reason: unit economics: W2 assistant payroll outran subscription revenue
summary: Subscription virtual-assistant service that converted contractors to employees; payroll burn exceeded revenue and the company shut down abruptly overnight in 2015 before an asset sale.

kill_reason: customer retention was terrible and 1099 misclassification lawsuits blocked further funding
summary: On-demand home-cleaning marketplace with heavy discounts that never converted to repeat customers; worker-classification lawsuits scared off investors. Shut down July 2015.

kill_reason: no willingness to pay: a $400 machine whose juice packs could be squeezed by hand
summary: Wi-Fi juice press with proprietary produce packs; once shown the packs could be hand-squeezed, the premium hardware value proposition collapsed. Shut down in 2017.

kill_reason: no demand for the format: premium short-form mobile-only video solved nobody's problem
summary: Raised $1.75B for mobile-only short premium video; launched into a market that already had free short video and shut down within six months in 2020.

kill_reason: subscription price far below cost of service; every active user deepened losses
summary: Unlimited theater tickets for $9.95/month while paying theaters full price; usage-heavy subscribers made losses scale with growth. Service collapsed in 2019.

kill_reason: unit economics: flat-fee on-demand pickup cost more to deliver than customers paid
summary: On-demand shipping pickup/packing for consumers; the $5 flat pickup never covered courier and packing costs and a late pivot to SMBs came too late. Shut down 2018.

kill_reason: thin-margin logistics with no moat; cheaper local incumbents undercut it
summary: On-demand laundry pickup/delivery; margins after courier costs were thin, retention weak, and local dry cleaners with delivery matched the offer. Shut down 2016.

kill_reason: full-stack meal production plus delivery never reached sustainable unit economics
summary: Cooked and delivered its own healthy meals; owning kitchens, couriers and demand generation made every order lose money at scale. Shut down 2017.

kill_reason: high production costs and heavy discounting; ran out of cash
summary: Chef-prepared meal delivery that burned through ~$125M; production overhead and discount-driven demand never converged on profitability. Closed January 2019.

kill_reason: cash-heavy inventory model with high burn; funding round collapsed
summary: Peer-to-peer used-car marketplace holding inventory risk; ~$7M/month burn and a failed strategic round killed it in 2017.

kill_reason: consumer-robotics hardware margins couldn't sustain the company between hit products
summary: Sold millions of Cozmo/Vector robots but hardware margins and seasonal sales couldn't cover R&D; a bridge financing fell through in 2019.

kill_reason: outpaced by cheap voice assistants; $899 social robot had no durable use case
summary: Social home robot crowdfunded to fame; by ship date, $50 smart speakers did the useful parts. Wound down from 2018.

kill_reason: the core technology never worked; fraud collapsed the company under regulatory action
summary: Claimed full blood panels from a finger prick; the proprietary devices could not deliver, and regulatory sanctions plus fraud charges dissolved the company in 2018.

kill_reason: squeezed between Fitbit and Apple; quality issues and litigation drained it
summary: Pioneer in fitness wearables that lost the category to better-capitalized rivals; liquidated in 2017 after burning ~$900M.

kill_reason: platform giants entered the category; independent hardware couldn't sustain scale
summary: Record-setting Kickstarter smartwatch maker; once Apple Watch and Android Wear shipped, sales and financing dried up. Assets sold to Fitbit in 2016.

kill_reason: anonymity attracted harassment; engagement collapsed after the novelty faded
summary: Anonymous sharing app that spiked then cratered as bullying dominated and retention vanished; shut down in 2015 and returned money to investors.

kill_reason: harassment-driven bans and de-anonymization updates destroyed usage
summary: Location-based anonymous campus feed valued near $400M; school bans plus product changes that removed anonymity collapsed engagement. Shut down 2017.

kill_reason: no creator monetization; talent and audience migrated to competitors
summary: Six-second video network that never paid creators; Instagram video and Snapchat pulled its stars away and Twitter shut it down in January 2017.

kill_reason: outmarketed by Spotify in a licensing-cost commodity market
summary: Well-designed music streaming service that couldn't win subscribers against Spotify's free tier and marketing; filed bankruptcy in 2015, assets to Pandora.

kill_reason: crowdsourced-invention model produced too many low-margin flops
summary: Community-invention platform that manufactured whatever the crowd voted for; scattershot low-volume products burned $185M. Bankruptcy in 2015.

kill_reason: spent like a giant on a product merchants hadn't agreed to buy
summary: UK mobile-payments 'unicorn' whose claimed merchant deals were mostly non-binding letters of intent; collapsed into administration in 2016.

kill_reason: great product, no distribution: growth too slow to raise the next round
summary: Beloved photo organization service with strong retention but tiny user base; couldn't out-distribute free offerings from platform giants. Shut down 2013.

kill_reason: killed by law: Supreme Court ruled its antenna-array retransmission infringed copyright
summary: Streamed broadcast TV via per-user micro-antennas; the entire model rested on a legal theory the Supreme Court rejected in 2014. Bankruptcy followed.

kill_reason: vertical integration in construction multiplied operational complexity faster than savings
summary: SoftBank-backed attempt to industrialize construction end-to-end; factories, projects and software all underperformed at once. Bankruptcy in 2021 after ~$2B raised.

kill_reason: burn wildly exceeded revenue; one-click checkout was a feature incumbents bundled
summary: One-click checkout startup spending ~$10M/month against ~$600k annual revenue; Shopify/Shop Pay and Bolt boxed it in. Shut down April 2022.

kill_reason: capital intensity: full autonomy stayed perpetually years away and backers stopped funding it
summary: Ford/VW-backed self-driving unit that consumed billions; when robotaxi timelines slipped again, both backers redirected capital to driver-assist. Closed 2022.

kill_reason: robot-made pizza never worked operationally; pivots couldn't outrun the burn
summary: Robotic pizza trucks (cheese slid off in transit) pivoted to sustainable packaging; ~$445M raised, shut down in 2023.

kill_reason: digital freight brokerage margins stayed razor-thin; freight recession plus frozen capital markets
summary: Digital freight marketplace valued at $3.8B; brokerage take rates never produced durable margins and the 2023 freight downturn ended it despite strong tech.

kill_reason: overpromised AI automation for hospitals; products underdelivered and churn followed
summary: Hospital back-office 'AI workforce' valued at $4B; internal reports found much automation was brittle RPA, customers churned, and the company wound down in 2023.

kill_reason: value-based care contracts lost money per patient; AI triage never carried the economics
summary: AI-triage plus telehealth provider that went public near $4B; capitated care costs exceeded revenue per member and the company collapsed into insolvency in 2023.

kill_reason: regulatory reclassification: converting 1099 caregivers to W2 destroyed the marketplace economics
summary: In-home senior-care marketplace; California worker-classification pressure forced a W2 model that erased its cost advantage over agencies. Shut down 2017.

kill_reason: reimbursement lagged reality: payers wouldn't sustainably pay for embedded tele-emergency care
summary: Placed first responders + telemedicine in nursing homes to avoid ER trips; demonstrated savings accrued to payers who wouldn't fund it. Shut down 2019.

kill_reason: software-plus-services law firm couldn't make legal work scale like software
summary: Tech-enabled law firm for startups ($75M raised); legal services stayed labor-bound and margin-poor despite the software layer. Wound down 2020.

kill_reason: CAC exceeded thin private-label margins; $3 products couldn't fund paid acquisition
summary: Direct-to-consumer everyday goods at flat low prices; customer-acquisition costs dwarfed contribution margin. First SoftBank Vision Fund company to fold, 2020.

kill_reason: burned through $118M switching between drone hardware and analytics as cheap hardware commoditized
summary: Enterprise drone platform that kept repositioning as DJI commoditized hardware; ran out of money in 2018 before analytics revenue matured.

kill_reason: no wedge into a saturated duopoly market despite $330M and a famous founder
summary: Andy Rubin's premium Android phone sold ~150k units into an Apple/Samsung market with zero switching pull; shut down in 2020.

kill_reason: fabricated traction: ~95% of claimed users were bots; trust and funding evaporated
summary: Event-discovery-turned-messaging app valued at $1.17B; a board investigation found the vast majority of its 20M 'users' were fake. Shut down 2023.

kill_reason: pivoted away from what worked; inventory-heavy redesign burned $300M+
summary: Design flash-sales site that hit a $1B valuation, then pivoted to holding inventory and conventional e-commerce; sold for parts by 2015.

kill_reason: commodity price collapse: polysilicon panels got cheaper than its novel design could ever be
summary: Cylindrical thin-film solar maker; crashing conventional panel prices erased its cost thesis. Bankruptcy in 2011 despite a $535M federal loan guarantee.

kill_reason: capital-intensive battery-swap network needed automaker standardization that never came
summary: Built battery-swap stations for EVs before EVs were common; only one car model ever supported it. Burned ~$850M, bankrupt 2013.

kill_reason: shipped ahead of component maturity; returns and platform giants' AirPods squeezed it
summary: Smart earbuds pioneer whose battery/Bluetooth compromises produced high return rates just as AirPods launched; closed 2017.
